---
title: "Cluster_Analysis"
author: "AhmedLubis"
date: "2026-05-16"
output: html_document
---

Preparation
```{r}
#reading_data
customer_data <- read.csv("D:/Kuliah/STA 2581 - Sains Data/STA 2581.Project/wholesale_customers_data.csv")

head(customer_data)
```

```{r}
#data_structure
str(customer_data)
summary(customer_data)
```

```{r}
#data_preparation
data_cluster <- customer_data[, c(
  "Fresh",
  "Milk",
  "Grocery",
  "Frozen",
  "Detergents_Paper",
  "Delicassen"
)]

head(data_cluster)
```
Exploration
```{r}
#scatterplot_matrix
pairs(data_cluster)
```
```{r}
#correlation
cor(data_cluster)
```
```{r}
#histogram
hist(data_cluster$Fresh,
     main = "Distribusi Fresh",
     xlab = "Fresh")
hist(data_cluster$Milk,
     main = "Distribusi Milk",
     xlab = "Milk")
hist(data_cluster$Grocery,
     main = "Distribusi Grocery",
     xlab = "Grocery")
hist(data_cluster$Frozen,
     main = "Distribusi Frozen",
     xlab = "Frozen")
hist(data_cluster$Detergents_Paper,
     main = "Distribusi Detergents_Paper",
     xlab = "Detergents_Paper")
hist(data_cluster$Delicassen,
     main = "Distribusi Delicassen",
     xlab = "Delicassen")
```
It can be seen that every histogram is right-skewed, so we have to transform
into log-form
```{r}
#all_histogram_right-skewed
#copy_og_data
data_log <- data_cluster
#log_transformation-(log_base_e): column 1-6
data_log[, 1:6] <- log(data_cluster[, 1:6] + 1)
```
Exploration data_log
```{r}
#scatterplot_matrix
pairs(data_log)
#correlation
cor(data_log)
```
```{r}
#histogram_log_form
#fresh
hist(data_log$Fresh,
     main = "Distribusi Fresh",
     xlab = "Fresh")
#milk
hist(data_log$Milk,
     main = "Distribusi Milk",
     xlab = "Milk")
#grocery
hist(data_log$Grocery,
     main = "Distribusi Grocery",
     xlab = "Grocery")
#frozen
hist(data_log$Frozen,
     main = "Distribusi Frozen",
     xlab = "Frozen")
#detergents_paper
hist(data_log$Detergents_Paper,
     main = "Distribusi Detergents_Paper",
     xlab = "Detergents_Paper")
#delicassen
hist(data_log$Delicassen,
     main = "Distribusi Delicasse",
     xlab = "Delicassen")
```
After we have done exploration data, we have to do standardization data
```{r}
#standardization_data
data_scaled <- scale(data_log)

head(data_scaled)
```
Next, the step should be done by us is measuring of distance
```{r}
#distance_measurement
#euclidean_distance
euclidean_distance <- dist(
  data_scaled,
  method = "euclidean"
)

as.matrix(euclidean_distance)[1:5,1:5]
#manhattan_distance
manhattan_distance <- dist(
  data_scaled,
  method = "manhattan"
)

as.matrix(manhattan_distance)[1:5,1:5]
#chebyshev_distance
chebyshev_distance <- dist(
  data_scaled,
  method = "maximum"
)

as.matrix(chebyshev_distance)[1:5,1:5]
```
```{r}
#based_on_max_spare
minkowski_distance <- dist(
  data_scaled,
  method = "minkowski",
  p = 3
)

as.matrix(minkowski_distance)[1:5,1:5]
```
After measure of distance, we will do cluster analysis
```{r}
#hierarchial_clustering
#complete_linkage
hc_complete <- hclust(
  euclidean_distance,
  method = "complete"
)

plot(
  hc_complete,
  hang = -1,
  main = "Dendrogram Complete Linkage"
)
```
```{r}
#creating_cluster
cluster_hc <- cutree(
  hc_complete,
  k = 5
)

cluster_hc
```
```{r}
#adding_cluster_to_data
customer_data$cluster_hc <- cluster_hc

head(customer_data)
```
```{r}
#cluster_visualization
library(factoextra)
```
```{r}
fviz_cluster(
  list(
    data = data_scaled,
    cluster = cluster_hc
  )
)
```
```{r}
library(dplyr)
```
```{r}
customer_data%>%
  group_by(cluster_hc) %>%
  summarise(
    Mean_fresh = mean(Fresh),
    Mean_milk = mean(Milk),
    Mean_grocery = mean(Grocery),
    Mean_frozen = mean(Frozen),
    Mean_detergents_paper = mean(Detergents_Paper),
    Mean_delicassen = mean(Delicassen)
  )
```
```{r}
#determine_optimal_number_of_cluster
#elbow_method
fviz_nbclust(
  data_scaled,
  kmeans,
  method = "wss"
)
#silhoutte_method
fviz_nbclust(
  data_scaled,
  kmeans,
  method = "silhouette"
)
```

Next step is k-means clustering. The "k" in k-means represents the number of 
clusters you want to find. The "means" refers to the average position of all
the data points within a cluster (the cluster center, or centroid).
```{r}
#k-means_clsutering
set.seed(123)

km <- kmeans(
  data_scaled,
  centers = 4,
  nstart = 25
)

km
```
```{r}
fviz_cluster(
  km,
  data = data_scaled
)
```
```{r}
#adding_the_result_of_cluster
customer_data$Cluster_KMeans <- km$cluster

head(customer_data)
```
```{r}
#cluster_evaluation
#calinski-harabasz_index
library(clusterSim)

index.G1(
  data_scaled,
  km$cluster
)
#davies-bouldin_index
index.DB(
  data_scaled,
  km$cluster
)$DB
```
```{r}
#comparison_clustering_model
# Complete linkage
hc_complete <- hclust(
  euclidean_distance,
  method = "complete"
)

# Average linkage
hc_average <- hclust(
  euclidean_distance,
  method = "average"
)

# Single linkage
hc_single <- hclust(
  euclidean_distance,
  method = "single"
)

# Ward linkage
hc_ward <- hclust(
  euclidean_distance,
  method = "ward.D2"
)
```
```{r}
#comparison_4clusters
cluster_complete <- cutree(hc_complete, k = 4)

cluster_average <- cutree(hc_average, k = 4)

cluster_single <- cutree(hc_single, k = 4)

cluster_ward <- cutree(hc_ward, k = 4)
```

Final step, we have to do some evaluations
```{r}
#evaluated_by_silhouette_index
library(cluster)

sil_complete <- silhouette(
  cluster_complete,
  euclidean_distance
)

sil_average <- silhouette(
  cluster_average,
  euclidean_distance
)

sil_single <- silhouette(
  cluster_single,
  euclidean_distance
)

sil_ward <- silhouette(
  cluster_ward,
  euclidean_distance
)

mean_complete <- mean(sil_complete[,3])

mean_average <- mean(sil_average[,3])

mean_single <- mean(sil_single[,3])

mean_ward <- mean(sil_ward[,3])

mean_complete

mean_average

mean_single

mean_ward
```
```{r}
#evaluated_by_davies-bouldin_index
library(clusterSim)

db_complete <- index.DB(
  data_scaled,
  cluster_complete
)$DB

db_average <- index.DB(
  data_scaled,
  cluster_average
)$DB

db_single <- index.DB(
  data_scaled,
  cluster_single
)$DB

db_ward <- index.DB(
  data_scaled,
  cluster_ward
)$DB

db_complete

db_average

db_single

db_ward
```
```{r}
#evaluated_by_calinski-harabasz_index
ch_complete <- index.G1(
  data_scaled,
  cluster_complete
)

ch_average <- index.G1(
  data_scaled,
  cluster_average
)

ch_single <- index.G1(
  data_scaled,
  cluster_single
)

ch_ward <- index.G1(
  data_scaled,
  cluster_ward
)

ch_complete

ch_average

ch_single

ch_ward
```
```{r}
#ringkasan_perbandingan_model
compared_result <- data.frame(
  Metode = c(
    "Complete",
    "Average",
    "Single",
    "Ward"
  ),

  Silhouette = c(
    mean_complete,
    mean_average,
    mean_single,
    mean_ward
  ),

  Davies_Bouldin = c(
    db_complete,
    db_average,
    db_single,
    db_ward
  ),

  Calinski_Harabasz = c(
    ch_complete,
    ch_average,
    ch_single,
    ch_ward
  )
)

compared_result
```

Determining the Optimal Number of Clusters

Based on the evaluation results above, the optimal number of clusters is 4. 
The reasons are as follows:

1. Elbow & Silhouette Method: I ran fviz_nbclust using the wss (Elbow) 
and silhouette methods. In the visual graph, I used 5 clusters, 
and it can be seen that some clusters still overlap, 
so I decided to use k=4 for K-Means.

2. K-Means Performance: The K-Means results with 4 clusters showed 
a Calinski-Harabasz Index of 131.36. This value is higher than 
all the hierarchical methods tested (Complete: 38.9, Average: 8.3, Ward: 108.6). 
A higher index indicates more well-separated clusters.

3. Davies-Bouldin Evaluation: The Davies-Bouldin Index for K-Means 
(k=4) was 1.69. In the hierarchical model comparison, although the Avg method 
has a very low DB value (0.35), it often produces unbalanced clusters or 
too few members in a single large group (chaining effect).

4. Cluster Size Balance: In K-Means with k=4, the cluster sizes are 130, 61, 
119, and 130. This distribution is quite even and facilitates business
interpretation compared to the Average or Single methods, which tend to group
almost all data into one large cluster.

Conclusion: I used 4 clusters with the K-Means clustering method because 
it provided the most stable clustering structure and had the highest internal
validity score (Calinski-Harabasz) among the tested models.
